# Co3O4@ β -cyclodextrin with synergistic peroxidase-mimicking performance as a signal magnification approach for colorimetric determination of ascorbic acid


Wenhui Lu, Jiaxin Zhang, Nianlu Li, Zhi You, Zhenyu Feng, Vinothkumar Natarajan, Jing Chen, Jinhua Zhan ⁎

Key Laboratory for Colloid & Interface Chemistry of Education Ministry, Department of Chemistry, Shandong University, Jinan250100, PR China


# A R T I C L E I N F O


Keywords: Cobalt oxide β -cyclodextrin Nanoparticle Peroxidase-mimicking Colorimetric sensor Ascorbic acid


# 1. Introduction


Natural enzymes have high efficiency and specificity towards substrates in biological systems [1]. The application of natural enzymes is limited because the intrinsic disadvantages of natural enzymes include high cost, low stability and difficulty in storage [2]. Therefore, biomimetic enzymes with similar properties as natural enzymes were generated [3]. Nanozymes have recently drawn more attention owing to its high stability and easy accessibility [4]. Since thefirst nanozyme possessed with natural peroxidase-mimicking property was reported in2007 [5], various nanozymes was discovered with natural enzymatic performance and exhibited overwhelming achievements in biosensor [6 -9], environmental protection [10], and clinical medicine [11].

Co3O4, as a type of nanozyme, has a higher reduction potential of Co (III)/Co(II) (1.92eV) than other metal ions [12]. Co3O4nanomaterials exhibited both catalase-like and peroxidase-like properties and also applied for the colorimetric biomolecule detection [13 -15]. However, nanozymes usually have low activity and poor specificity with substrates, which was affected by diverse crystallized nanostructures and

⁎

Corresponding author. E-mail address:


# A B S T R A C T


Transition metal oxides nanoparticles have shown enzyme-mimetic performance. It was demonstrated that the Co3O4@ β -cyclodextrin nanoparticles (Co3O4@ β -CD NPs) showed a higher peroxidase-mimicking catalytic activity than pure Co3O4NPs. β -CD molecules were coated onto the surface of Co3O4NPs via a one-pot synthesis method. Co3O4@ β -CD NPs displayed higher affinity to3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) through MichaelisMenten mechanism after modification with β -CD. The catalytic efficiency of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs for TMB oxidation was9.5-fold higher than that of the pure Co3O4NPs because of the effective host-guest interactions between Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and TMB. Further, the hydrophobic cavity of β -CD while hosting TMB could reduce the transfer distance of electrons between TMB and Co3O4catalyst, which could lead to faster oxidation of TMB. The synergistic effects between Co3O4and β -CD enhanced the peroxidase-mimicking property. The efficient and visual colorimetric determination of Ascorbic Acid (AA) assay was achieved. The limit of detection was1.09 μ M (S/N = 3) and the linear range was10 -60 μ M based on the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs peroxidase-mimicking-mediated3.0-fold signal magnification strategy. Moreover, the outstanding selectivity toward the detection of AA was achieved, which could exhibit prospects in biomedical analysis.

multifarious surface configuration [16,17]. Therefore, the construction of nanozyme with high activity, stability and affinity to the target substrates is still a great challenge [1,16,18].

In recent years, various nanozymes were discovered with natural enzymes performance and exhibited excellent colorimetric performance in biosensorfields [19]. The mechanism of colorimetric approach is replied on the catalytic activity between catalysts and substances, the color changes can be observed by naked eyes [20]. The adsorption of substances and electron transfer between catalysts and substances pay a key role in colorimetric approach [21]. Hence, The catalytic activity of colorimetric approach was influenced by surface microenvironment, such as surface electrostatic, hydrophilic and hydrophobic interactions [16,22]. In order to achieve a high activity in nanozyme in the application of colorimetric detection, surface modification could be introduced with various active groups by enhancing the affinity of colorimetric substrates [23]. β -cyclodextrin ( β -CD) is a cyclic oligosaccharide comprised of seven glucose units with a hydrophobic central cavity and hydrophilic outer surface [24]. For example, β -CD modified with gold nanoparticles have shown activated cascade jhzhan@sdu.edu.cn (J. Zhan).

reactions to glucose oxidase-like and horseradish peroxidase-mimicking [17]. The surface modification of nanoparticles by β -CD could not only offer synergistic nanomaterials but also expect to achieve novel performances, functions, and applications [25,26]. However, the mechanism of peroxidase-mimicking by β -CD modification on the surface of nanoparticles requires to explore and the colorimetric method needs to develop based on β -CD modified with nanoparticles [17].

In this study, we developed a facile and room temperature preparation method to develop Co3O4@ β -CD NPs for mimicking peroxidase. β -CD effectively modified Co3O4NPs and promoted the enzymelike activity. The results exhibited that the peroxidase-mimicking property of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was superior to pure Co3O4NPs by catalytically oxidizing3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with H2O2and generated a prominent colorimetric change. It was verified that β -CD with specific topology structure could enhance the affinity with TMB and absorb more TMB molecules to produce host-guest complex. The absorption of TMB could shorten the diffusion distance and enhance the reaction efficiency in peroxidase-mimicking reaction. In addition, Ascorbic Acid (AA) has antioxidant properties and occurs naturally, which could reduce oxidized-TMB to TMB through the twoelectron transfer process and could be visualized by blue color fading. Herein, a signal magnification colorimetric approach to detect AA by monitoring the color change was achieved.


# 2. Experimental section



# 2.1. Chemicals


All theChemicals were illustrated in Supporting Information.


# 2.2. Synthesis of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs


Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O (2.5g) was dissolved in200mL water with vigorous stirring under oxygen atmosphere. After20min stirring, 50mL of3.5M NaOH solution (including2.0g β -CD) was added drop-wise within15min at ambient temperature. Then, 24h stirring procedure was carried out to obtain thefinal black reaction mixtures. Thefinal black reaction mixtures, hereafter, appeared as viscous and slurry-like, which was centrifugated at speed of4000rpm. The mixture was then washed and dried at60 °C to obtain Co3O4@ β -CD NPs, and sintered the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs in the air at200 °C for4h to obtain pure Co3O4NPs.


# 2.3. Characterization techniques


All the characterization techniques were illustrated in Supporting Information.


# 2.4. Peroxidase-mimicking performance of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs


In the peroxidase-mimicking assay, 1.5mL of TMB-HCl solution and500 μ L of H2O2with a constant concentration were added into HAcNaAc buffer solution with a pH value of4.4. Then, 100 μ L of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs suspension (1mg mL -1 ) was mixed into the above solution. The absorbance of TMB ( ε 652nm = 39,000M -1cm -1 ) after incubation for10min at25 °C was detected by UV -vis measurement byfixing wavelength at652nm in a spectrum and time mode. The effect of pH and temperature was also investigated. The robustness and storage stability was studied by incubating the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs in a refrigerator at4 °C for two months.

The Michaelis-Menten kinetic assay could obtain the plot of initial reaction velocity v versus substrates (TMB or H2O2) concentration at a constant amount of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. Besides, the Lineweaver-Burk plot was depicted to better understand the kinetic mechanism and accurate estimate the Michaelis-Menten constant and the maximal reaction velocity. The equation of apparent kinetic parameters is as follows:

where v is the original reaction rate and Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity observed at saturating substrate concentration; [S] is the concentration of the substrate and Km is the Michaelis constant. Km represents the affinity of the nanozyme for its substrate and is defined as the substrate concentration at half of the maximum velocity. The peroxidase-mimicking catalytic efficiency is defined by ∕ k cat Km , where kcat is the catalytic constant, = ∕ k V [E] cat max .


# 2.5. Detection of AA


AA with different concentration was added to HAc-NaAc buffer solution of pH4.4consisting of a certain concentration of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs suspension, H2O2and TMB. The concentration range of AA was varied from0to1.0mM. The absorbance of the solution was detected at652nm after incubating15min. The absorbance change ( Δ A) is calculated by A(652nm, AA)-A(652nm, absence). The limit of detection was calculated from the linear of the calibration plot, the equation is calculated by KS /m b , where K is equal to3, Sb is the standard deviation of the blank and m is the slope of the calibration plot [27].

The standard analysis method was GBT15347-2015, China, which was described in Supporting Information.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Morphology, structure and surface species


High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs were shown in Fig. 1a and b. The morphology of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs showed well dispersed nanoparticles in the size of10nm. The latticeresolved HRTEM image was shown in Fig. 1a, which was marked by the solid rectangular area. A lattice fringe of0.28nm corresponds to the (220) plane and0.46nm corresponds to the (111) plane of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs [28]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and pure Co3O4NPs were shown in Fig. 1c, which exhibited spinel-type structure (PDF#042-1467) with a space group of Fd3m [29]. The crystal sizes of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and Co3O4NPs, estimated from spinel (311) plane (at2 θ =36.96°) was8.29nm and6.77nm by DebyeScherrer equation. The diffraction peaks at18.95°, 31.39°, 36.96°, 38.21°, 44.77°, 59.54° and65.27° can be assigned to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (511), and (440) reflection. The crystal structures diffraction peaks confirmed the presence of a pure Co3O4phase without Co(OH)2or other impurities. The Raman spectra of the Co3O4NPs and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs were shown in Fig. S1. Among thefive evident Raman peaks, the peaks at194, 522and626cm -1were related to3F2g mode. The peaks observed at482and691cm -1correspond to1Eg, and1A1g Raman active modes, respectively. A1g and F2g was a Co-O stretching mode and contributed to cobalt ions in the coordinated sites [30]. The X-ray diffraction (XPS) spectra of the Co2p peak for the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and Co3O4NPs were shown in Fig. 1d. Co2p3/2consist of Co3+ (ca. 779.8eV) and Co2+ (ca. 795.2eV) and the relative atomic ratio of Co2+ / Co3+ (1.6) for Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and Co3O4NPs was same [31]. The energy difference between the Co2p1/2and Co2p3/ 2peaks for Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was15.4eV, which agreed with previous report [32].

To investigate the crystal structure formation during synthesis process, the XRD patterns were applied to illustrate the preparation mechanism of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. As shown in Fig. S2, after three hours synthesis reaction, the crystallographic structure had a brucite-like phase ( β -Co(OH)2) with crystalline size of10.389nm, which was the pivotal precursor for preparing the spinel Co3O4 [33]. The cobalt ions center would be coordinated to two oxygen atoms of adjacent hydroxyl groups from deprotonation of β -CD [34 -36]. With oxygen molecules participation, the above β -CD-wrapped layered crystallites were

Fig. 1. (a,b) HRTEM and TEM images of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. (c) XRD patterns of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and Co3O4NPs. (d) Co2p XPS spectra of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and Co3O4NPs.

transformed into Co3O4@ β -CD NPs gradually, which were stably dispersed in solution.

In order to analyze the function of the oxygen molecules and β -CD on preparation process, different sets of experiments (including without oxygen molecules and without β -CD) were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. S3, without oxygen molecules, the β -Co(OH)2phase had exhibited thin sheet shape, hence further oxidation would not happen. In addition, the HCo (III) O2as an over-oxidation production was generated in the absence of β -CD [37]. From above, it could deduce two factors that may be attributable to the low-temperature preparation of spinel Co3O4. First, β -CD can prevent over-oxidation process, and serve as a scaffold molecule. When vicinal cobalt ions were introduced, it can produce stabilized complexes of cobalt ions with β -CD [38]. Second, the hydroxyl groups of β -CD could act as the reducing sites for the reduction of Co3+ ions and the hydroxyl groups were self-oxidized into carboxylic acid, which could be proved by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) from the wavelength of1631cm -1and1383cm -1in Fig. 2a.

To demonstrate that the β -CD was modified on the surface of Co3O4, the FT-IR spectra of Co3O4and Co3O4@ β -CD were shown in Fig. 2a for comparison. Among them, ˜ 575cm -1and ˜ 662cm -1were the characteristic peaks of Co3O4spinel oxides [39]. The peak located on ˜ 12001000cm -1was assigned to the structure of β -CD, which were the symmetrical stretching of glycosidic C -O-C and C -O bonds in polysaccharides. These peaks indicated the successful modification of β -CD on the surface of Co3O4 [39,40]. Furthermore, the Thermo Gravimetric Analyzer (TGA) curves of Co3O4and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was displayed in Fig. 2b. Before200 °C, thefirst weight loss stage was due to the removal of water and ethanol during the synthesis process. The main weight loss stage in the range of200 -400 °C can be attributed to the removal of β -CD. The significant weight loss of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and Co3O4NPs was

9.59and3.14% respectively. The content of β -CD modified on the surface of Co3O4was approximately6.45%. As shown in Fig. 2c, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs had approximately20.58% C -OX (C -O-C & C -OH) species higher than Co3O4NPs contained11.58% in Fig. S4a. According to FT-IR spectra results, β -CD modified on the surface of Co3O4with amounts of hydroxyl groups could lead to the increase of C -OX species in Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. The peak at288.84eV shifted to288.4eV due to stronger ionic interactions between hydroxyl anion and cobalt cations on Co3O4@ β -CD NPs surfaces in Figs. 2c and S4a [41]. Further, the relative percentage of the OC component gradually decreases and the peak at532.8eV was due to the Co-O-C bonds between Co3O4and β -CD (Figs. 2d and S4b) [42]. Above results confirmed the presence of β -CD on the surface of Co3O4NPs.


# 3.2. Evaluation of peroxidase mimetic activity of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs


In order to investigate the peroxidase-mimicking activity of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs, the activity of representative peroxidase colorimetric substrate TMB was reacted with H2O2. A prominent oxidation reaction occurred to generate blue color change in the maximum absorption peak at approximately652nm when TMB, H2O2, and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs were mixed together for incubation as shown in Fig. 3a. Moreover, no obvious color changes phenomenon of the systems appeared when any one of the three parts were not present. The results illustrated that Co3O4@ β -CD NPs had peroxidase-mimicking properties. Besides, the peroxidase activity of β -CD, Co3O4and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was investigated (Fig. 3b). The results proved that Co3O4@ β -CD NPs showed much higher activity than β -CD and Co3O4. Furthermore, time-dependent absorption spectra of the different systems also indicated the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs exhibited better peroxidase-mimicking performance within10min (Fig. S5). Above results manifested that the synergistic

Fig. 2. (a) FT-IR spectra of Co3O4NPs, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and β -CD. (b) TGA curves of Co3O4NPs and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. (c, d) C1s and O1s XPS spectra of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs.

effect promoted the peroxidase-mimicking activity of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs efficiently. In addition, optimal experiments for different amounts of β -CD on the surface of Co3O4were conducted. As shown in Fig. S6a, TGA analysis was illustrated that different β -CD percentage amounts were coated on the surface of Co3O4. The peroxidase-mimicking activity of Co3O4modified with different β -CD percentage amounts was shown in Fig. S6b. The peroxidase-mimicking activity was increased with increasing β -CD amounts due to more TMB absorbed on the cavity of β -CD and enhanced the electron transfer. The catalytic activity was optimum when the β -CD percentage amount was approximately6.45%. Besides, the peroxidase-mimicking activity was decreased when the β -

CD amount over6.45%, which due to more β -CD molecules would shield the active sites of Co3O4. So, Co3O4@ β -CD with approximately6.45% β -CD amount was chosen as optimal peroxidase mimicking nanozyme.

The peroxidase-mimicking activity was influenced by pH, temperature and substrate concentration in the reaction system. The effects of concentration and reaction environment factors were detected. When incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer at ambient temperature (25 °C), the optimal pH and temperature were pH4.4and45 °C, respectively (Fig. S7a,b). Co3O4@ β -CD NPs were shown better peroxidase-mimicking activity than pure Co3O4. Furthermore, Co3O4NPs needed three orders

Fig. 3. (a) Typical absorption spectra of the different solutions. (b) Relative activity of the different systems: A-TMB + Co3O4@ β -CD NPs +H2O2; B-TMB + Co3O4NPs +H2O2; C-TMB+ β -CD+H2O2.

of TMB input load than Co3O4@ β -CD NPs to attain the maximal activity of peroxidase-mimicking reaction as shown in Fig. S7c. In addition, when the concentration of H2O2was increased, the peroxidase-mimicking activity was inhibited because the reactive sites were saturated. After modified with β -CD, the higher catalytic activity could be observed as shown in Fig. S7c,d. In order to exclude the influence of leaching solution in peroxidase-mimicking activity system, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs were incubated in the pH4.4for30min, then the nanoparticles were removed by centrifugation from solution. The peroxidase-mimicking activity of the leaching solution and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was compared under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S8, the leaching of cobalt ions had no activity, which indicated that the observed peroxidase-mimicking activity was assigned to Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. As shown in Fig. S9, the particles of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs were monodisperse with nearly identical diameters before and after incubation. The results indicated that the peroxidase-mimicking activity measurement (i.e., incubation under pH4.4reaction condition for600s) did not influence the stability of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. The size distribution of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs after three consecutive runs under similar reaction conditions was detected as shown in Fig. S10. The size distribution values were114.6, 105.7and158.0nm, respectively. The value of reused Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was much close to that of initial Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. Besides, the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was monitored after being stored for two months in a refrigerator. As shown in Fig. S11, the relative activity of peroxidasemimicking was no apparent decrease after two months, which proved good storage stability of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs as peroxidase mimic.


# 3.3. Steady-state kinetic assay and Reaction mechanism


To illustrate the peroxidase-mimicking reaction kinetics of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs, the enzyme kinetics evaluation for the reaction were investigated. As illustrated in Figs. S12a-d and S13, Michaelis -Menten kinetics were detected for Co3O4@ β -CD NPs (Fig. S12a,c) and Co3O4 (Fig. S12b,d), the results illustrated that the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs exhibited rate-saturation kinetics and obeyed the Michaelis-Menten equation. The reaction velocity v was controlled by substrate concentration when limited quantities of active sites were saturated by substrates [43]. The lower Km value represented higher affinity towards substrates [43]. As shown in Table S1, the Km values of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs with H2O2or TMB substrate were smaller than pure Co3O4NPs due to the modification of β -CD. The Km values (0.17mM) for TMB of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was smaller than1.5-fold that of the pure Co3O4NPs (0.25mM). Because of lower Km, the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs had TMB catalytic efficiency ( ∕ k K cat m ) of7.59 × 10 3M -1s -1 , 9.5-fold higher than that of the pure Co3O4NPs (8.00 × 10 2M -1s -1 ). Moreover, the Km value of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was similar to the Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP) from Fig. S14and Table S1and lower than other nanomaterials in Table S1. The results suggested that Co3O4@ β -CD NPs as a kind of nanozyme exhibited excellent peroxidase-mimicking catalytic efficiency.

The ping-pong mechanism of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was investigated by detecting the reaction activity over a series of different concentration of substrate. As shown in Fig. S12e,f, the parallel-line data in LineweaverBurk plots were proved the ping-pong kinetic mechanism [44]. Co3O4@ β -CD NPs as a kind of nanozyme processed single substrate binding sites, bind and react with thefirst substrate, after reaction releasing thefirst product and subsequently reacting with the second substrate.


# 3.4. The inclusion complexes formation between Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and TMB


To understand the synergistic effect of Co3O4and β -CD in peroxidase-mimicking catalysic reaction process, the interaction between β -CD and TMB was demonstrated by host-guest behavior. Fig. 4a showed the FT-IR spectra of adsorbed TMB on the surface of Co3O4@ β -CD, the sharp benzene ring skeleton stretching vibration at1479cm -1

diminished or shifted and the primary amine stretching vibrations disappeared in the inclusion complexes. The sharp peak at1479cm -1diminished which attributed to benzene ring skeleton stretching vibration. The primary amine deformation vibrations at1601cm -1disappeared due to the formation of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and TMB complex. The peaks appeared at2856and1378cm -1were due to the C -H stretching and bending vibrations, which disappeared in the inclusion complexes. Besides, the existence of the intermolecular hydrogen bonding between the TMB and β -CD, the O e H stretching peak of β -CD shifted to a higher wavelength [45]. The FT-IR spectra confirmed the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs and TMB inclusion complexes were formed. Circular dichroism (CD) could induce the absorption bands of the achiral guest molecules, which formed in the cavity of chiral β -CD [46]. As shown in Fig. S15, TMB exhibited mild signal in the250 -300nm region, where below250nm a positive CD signal was found and no signal existed in other contrast groups in Fig. 4b. The CD results further indicated the complex formed between TMB and β -CD.

Combing the Ping Pong mechanism of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs, the formation of complex intermediates could facilitate peroxidase-mimicking catalysis reaction. The Co3O4@ β -CD NPs active sites could bind and transfer electron between TMB and H2O2. Besides, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs with TMB complex intermediates could facilitate electron transfer and shorten the topologically distant of active sites.


# 3.5. Detection AA by using Co3O4@ β -CD NPs


The capability of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs to determine AA was shown in Scheme1. As shown in Fig. 5a,c, the results exhibited that the absorbance of the system was decreased quickly with different concentration of AA. AA could be determined as low as1.09 μ M with a linear range from10to60 μ M in Fig. 5b and superior other detection methods as shown in Table S2. Besides, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs detection had3.0-fold higher (Abs. = 0.55) than that of pure Co3O4NPs (Abs. = 0.18). Hence, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs mediated oxTMB based detection system could perform as signal magnification method. To verify the selectivity of this method toward AA determination, the effect of other interfering substances with AA were investigated in Fig. 5d. When the concentration of coexisting substances was increased20times than AA solution, the absorbance showed no prominent changes. The results indicated Co3O4@ β -CD NPs had a good specificity to AA.

The detection mechanism of AA was that Co3+ fi rst directly obtained an electron from the TMB and reduced into Co2+ , making TMB oxidized into a blue oxTMB product. Then Co2+ lost the electron to H2O2and turned back to Co3+ [20]. The reducibility of AA origining from the phenol hydroxyl group in the structure, which could induce the reduction of oxTMB to TMB. In addition, β -CD modified on the surface of Co3O4was vital for absorbing substances to shorten the topological distance between active sites and result in quickly sensing response. After modified with β -CD due to the host-guest interaction with TMB, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs shown high affinity toward TMB and H2O2, which induced fast electron transfer between TMB and Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. Hence, the synergistic function of β -CD and Co3O4provided excellent AA detection performance.


# 3.6. Reproducibility and repeatability of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs for AA detection


The reproducibility and repeatability of the detection AA were conducted by preparing Co3O4@ β -CD NPs solution at different time. Seven different batches (six per batch) of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs solution were made at different time by applying the same experimental condition. The relative standard deviations (RSD) value of the same batch Co3O4@ β -CD NPs solution was5% (n = 6, same batch) and the RSD value of different batches Co3O4@ β -CD NPs solution was6% (n = 7, different batches). Thus the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was found to exhibit better reproducibility and the repeatability for determination AA.

Fig. 4. (a) FT-IR spectra of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs + TMB and TMB. (b) Circular Dichroism spectrum Co3O4@ β -CD NPs + TMB.


# 3.7. Co3O4@ β -CD NPs for real sample AA detection


To prove the practicability for real sample AA detection, Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was used to determinate the AA concentration and evaluate the repeatability in Vitamin C tablet sample in room temperature condition. The detection AA content was105.9mg/g, which was near the label amount from pharmaceutical instruction (126.6mg/g). Besides, in order to investigate the precision of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs detection method, recovery experiments were made by adding a serious of AA solution with different concentration. As shown in Table S3, the average recovery of all the samples was range from96.8% to113.0%. The result determined by Co3O4@ β -CD NPs colorimetric method was near the detection result of the standard analysis method, which indicated the colorimetric detection of Co3O4@ β -CD NPs could be applied to detection AA in real samples.


# 4. Conclusion


In summary, the β -cyclodextrin modified Co3O4NPs was prepared via facile one-pot room temperature oxidation strategy. β -CD molecules play an important role as both the capping agent and reducing agent during synthesis process. The peroxidase-mimicking performance of the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs was demonstrated to be ˜ 9.5-fold higher than the pure Co3O4. The interface reaction mechanism was that TMB could form complexes with Co3O4@ β -CD NPs. Furthermore, the Co3O4@ β -CD NPs exhibited broad application to sensitively detect AA with a low limit of detection of1.09 μ M (S/N = 3) and showed ˜ 3.0-fold signal magnification. This work provided a modified strategy to greatly develop the peroxidase-mimicking properties in order to expand the applications of Co3O4NPs in colorimetric sensing.

Wenhui Lu (China, 1993) is a PhD student at the Department of Chemistry, Shandong University, Jinan. She received her B.S. from Ludong University. Her PhD is in Analytical Chemistry. Her current research is focused on understanding the mechanism of enzymelike reaction and designing functional nanozyme catalysts for analytical detection.

Jiaxin Zhang is a B.S. student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, China. she is focused on the synthesis and application of nanoparticles with peroxidase-like performance.

Nianlu Li is a PhD student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, China. She obtained M.S. from Liaocheng University. Now she is mainly working on the development of functional nanomaterials for the application of enzymelike detection.

Zhi You is a B.S. student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, China. He has ever worked on the exploration of peroxidase-like activity based on nanocomposites.

Zhenyu Feng is a full engineer in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, China. He is currently collaborating research with Prof. Jinhua Zhan ' s group and mainly focuses on analytical detection via designing functional nanosized materials.

Vinothkumar Natarajan is a post-doctor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, China. He is focused on development of peroxidaselike nanomaterials via adjusting the structures of function nanomaterials in Prof. Jinhua Zhan ' s group.

Jing Chen is an associate professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, China. She received her PhD degree in Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. She has ever worked on the analytical detection via sensors based on functional nanomaterials.

Jinhua Zhan received the Ph.D. degree from University of Science and Technology of China in2000. Then he became a postdoctoral fellow at National Taiwan University. He joined the National Institute for Materials Science as a special researcher in2003. He became a full professor of chemistry at Shandong University in2006. So far, he has more than150papers published in the international peer-reviewed journals, being cited more than5800times. His h-index reaches43. His current research interests include nanomaterials synthesis and their applications for sensing.


# Appendix. Supplementary data



# Co3O4@ β -cyclodextrin with synergistic peroxidase-mimicking activity as a signal magnification approach for colorimetric determination of Ascorbic Acid


Wenhui Lu, Jiaxin Zhang, Nianlu Li, Zhi You, Zhenyu Feng, Vinothkumar Natarajan, Jing Chen, Jinhua Zhan*

Key Laboratory for Colloid & Interface Chemistry of Education Ministry, Department of Chemistry, Shandong University, Jinan250100, P.R. China


# Chemicals


β -Cyclodextrin ( β -CD, ≥96%), Sodium acetate (NaAc, 99%), 3,3′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine HCl (TMB), Peroxidase from horseradish (HRP, >300units/mg) , The above chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3 ) 2·H2O,  98.5%),  Sodium  hydroxide  (NaOH,  96.0%), L-Ascorbic acid (AA, ≥99.7%), Potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.5%), Sodium bromide (NaBr, ≥99%), Acetic acid  (HAc,  ≥99.5%) were  obtained  from  Sinopharm  Chemical  Reagent  Co.,  Ltd. Proline  (99%),  Potassium  phosphate  monobasic  (KH2PO4 ,  99.5%), Alanine  (99%), Lysine (98%), Fructose (99%), Maltose monohydrate (97%), Galactose (99%) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Vitamin C tablet was obtained from  CSPC  Pharma. All  the  solutions  were  prepared  using  deionized  water  with  a resistance of18.25MΩ cm.


# Characterization techniques


Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7700) and high -resolution transmission  electron  microscopy  (HRTEM,  JEM2100)  were  used  to  observe  the morphology of all  the  materials.  Fourier  transformed  infrared  (FTIR)  spectra  were produced by Bruker VERTEX70s pectrometer and used to prove chemical bonds about the β -CD on the surface of Co3O4NPs. Xray diffractometer (Bruker D8) with Cu Kα (λ = 0.1542nm) radiation was used to detect the X -ray diff raction (XRD) patterns in the range of2080°. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was condu cted on a thermal analyzer (TGA/SDTA, 851e METTLER) under air atmosphere at a heating rate of10o Cmin -1 .  Circular  dichroism  (CD ) spectra  were  obtained  by  using  a  JASCO  J810spectropolarimeter.  Dynamic  light  scattering  analyzer  (DLS,  Zetasizer  NanoZS, Malvern ) was used to detect the particle size distribution of nanoparticles. X-ray Photoelectronic Spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250) measurement was adopted to characterize  the  chemical  composition  of  the  Co3O4NPs.  Horiba  LabRAM HR800Evolution apparatus with a633n m laser was used as the excitation source to conduct Raman  characterization.  UV-Vis  (PERSEE  TU1810)  measurement  was  used  to monitor the absorbance changes during the peroxidase-mimicking reaction process.


# Standard analysis method


The standard analysis method was based on GBT15347 -2015, China. Firstly, 0.3g of real sample was dissolved in80mL deionized water a nd then added2mL H2SO4 (20%) with vigorous stirring. Secondly, iodine standard titration solution was used to titrate the above solution. When approaching the titration endpoint, the starch indicator solution was added and sequentially titrated until the solution turn to blue. The titration process was finished after30s.
